whatToFeed: High phosphorus and potassium tomato feed once fruit sets.
pruningTips: Pinch out suckers emerging from the leaf axils of indeterminate varieties.
flavorProfile: Sweet, bright, and slightly tart bursting with juice.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Start seeds indoors mid-March; transplant outdoors late May.
whenToHarvest: When fruits are fully colored and yield slightly to gentle pressure.
usesForLargeHarvests: Roasting, canning, making tomato paste, sun-drying.
plantingInstructions: Sow seeds indoors 1/4 inch deep in seed-starting formula 6-8 weeks before the last frost. Maintain soil temperatures between 70-75°F for quick germination. Hardening off seedlings for a week before transplanting outside 18-24 inches apart is crucial.
growthRequirements: Demands highly fertile, deep, well-draining loam packed with rich compost and a stable pH level of 6.0 to 6.8. Requires constant soil dampness to prevent split skins, paired with continuous, direct all-day summer sun.

whatToFeed: Calcium-rich tomato fertilizer to prevent blossom end rot.
pruningTips: Prune lightly; as a determinate bush variety, they only need lower leaves cleared for airflow.
flavorProfile: Meaty, dense, and low-acid with deep savory tomato flavor.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Start seeds indoors mid-March; transplant outdoors mid-to-late May.
whenToHarvest: Harvest when uniformly deep red and slightly soft to the touch.
usesForLargeHarvests: Canning, making marinara or pizza sauce, freezing whole, dehydrating.
plantingInstructions: Sow seeds indoors in small pots 6-8 weeks before the final spring frost date. Bury seeds 1/4 inch deep and use a heat mat to speed growth. Plant the starts outdoors up to their lowest leaves, spacing them 12-18 inches apart in a row.
growthRequirements: Thrives best in loose, organic-rich soil with a slightly acidic to neutral pH of 6.2 to 6.8. Requires uniform moisture management to maximize thick wall development, along with intense full sun to set massive cluster flushes.

whatToFeed: Heavy organic compost supplemented with high-potassium liquid feed.
pruningTips: Maintain a single main stem by aggressively removing all side suckers.
flavorProfile: Rich, classic, old-fashioned tomato flavor with a perfect acid balance.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Start seeds indoors early-to-mid March; transplant outdoors late May after soil warms.
whenToHarvest: Harvest when fully colored and heavy, right before they become too soft.
usesForLargeHarvests: Tomato soup, thick salsa batches, juicing, stewed tomatoes for canning.
plantingInstructions: Start seeds indoors 8 weeks before the last expected frost date. Plant out in late May once night temperatures remain safely above 50°F. Bury the long leggy stems deep into the planting crater to trigger extensive secondary root growth.
growthRequirements: Demands exceptionally fertile, deep, well-aerated sandy loam with a pH profile of 6.0 to 6.8. Requires deep, continuous watering routines combined with a heavy cage or trellis system. Must have unchecked, scorching full sun.

whatToFeed: Balanced 5-5-5 organic fertilizer early on, shifting to low-nitrogen high-potassium once flowering.
pruningTips: Pinch off the main growing tip when it reaches the top of the trellis to encourage lateral fruiting branches.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Direct sow outdoors mid-to-late May once soil temperature hits 65°F.
whenToHarvest: Pick when firm, green, and roughly 6-8 inches long before they turn yellow.
usesForLargeHarvests: Relish, quick pickles, cucumber gazpacho, infused water, cucumber salad.
plantingInstructions: Sow seeds directly into warm outdoor beds 1 inch deep, spacing seeds 6 inches apart down a structural line, or plant in distinct warm dirt hills with 3 seeds per cluster. Thin to one robust vine every 12 inches.
growthRequirements: Requires rich, deeply cultivated, well-draining garden beds heavily fortified with leaf compost and a pH range of 6.0 to 6.8. High moisture parameters must be sustained uniformly; erratic drying out leads to intensely bitter fruit chunks.

whatToFeed: High-potassium fertilizer once flowering begins to boost fruit development.
pruningTips: Remove damaged or lower leaves touching the ground to improve air circulation and prevent mildew.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Direct sow or transplant outdoors late May when danger of frost has fully passed.
whenToHarvest: Harvest when skin is tender and glossy, ideally at 6-8 inches long.
usesForLargeHarvests: Freezing slices, squash bread/muffins, pickling, ratatouille, grilling.
plantingInstructions: Sow seeds directly outdoors in late May 1 inch deep in small mounds spaced 3 feet apart. If starting indoors, use plantable peat pots 3 weeks prior to prevent transplant root shock when sliding them into open soil fields.
growthRequirements: Thrives best in warm, rich, organic sandy loam soil that drains well, maintaining a neutral pH of 6.0 to 6.8. Demands large amounts of root moisture to match massive fan foliage sweat rates, combined with open full sun.

whatToFeed: Heavy feeder; apply well-rotted manure and balanced organic fertilizer.
pruningTips: Trim massive fan leaves that block sunlight from developing fruit or neighboring plants.
flavorProfile: Mild, watery, and versatile with a delicate sweetness.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Direct sow or plant transplants outdoors from late May to early June.
whenToHarvest: Pick frequently when fruits are 5-7 inches long to encourage continuous production.
usesForLargeHarvests: Shredding and freezing for baking, zucchini bread, zoodles, relish, dehydrating.
plantingInstructions: Sow seeds directly outdoors in late spring once soil layers reach 65°F. Plant 1 inch deep, dropping 2 seeds per cluster every 2-3 feet. Thin to the single most vigorous sprout after first true serrated leaflets fully map out.
growthRequirements: Requires rich, heavily composted, highly porous loam that stays moist without retaining pools, keeping a pH between 6.0 and 7.5. Demands widespread square footage to branch, along with high-heat full sun exposures.

whatToFeed: Organic fertilizer rich in magnesium and potassium; avoid excess nitrogen.
pruningTips: Pinch out the early first blossoms to encourage the plant to put energy into a stronger root system and structural branch system.
flavorProfile: Crisp, firm, and distinctly grassy with a mild herbal bite.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Start seeds indoors mid-March; transplant outdoors late May when night temps stay above 55°F.
whenToHarvest: Harvest when fruits are full size, firm, and deep green.
usesForLargeHarvests: Freezing diced pieces, stuffed peppers, pickling, fajita mix prep.
plantingInstructions: Sow seeds indoors 8 weeks before the terminal frost date, planting 1/4 inch deep in seed cells. Keep seedlings under bright grow lights. Set outside in late May, spacing plants 12-18 inches apart in sunny rows.
growthRequirements: Prefers lightweight, crumbly, well-draining garden loams loaded with trace minerals and maintaining a pH of 6.2 to 6.8. Needs consistent soil moisture levels to curb flower drop, matched with relentless bright sun warmth.

whatToFeed: Organic fertilizer rich in magnesium and potassium; avoid excess nitrogen.
pruningTips: Pinch out the early first blossoms to encourage the plant to put energy into a stronger root system and structural branch system.
flavorProfile: Deeply sweet, juicy, and crisp with subtle fruity undertones.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Start seeds indoors mid-March; transplant outdoors late May.
whenToHarvest: Leave on the vine until the green skin turns fully brilliant red for maximum sweetness.
usesForLargeHarvests: Roasted red peppers, home canning, freezing, hot sauce, dehydrating into pepper powder.
plantingInstructions: Start seeds indoors mid-March using a seed tray and seedling heat mat to sustain 80°F warmth. Once true leaves sprout, pot up to larger cups. Move outdoors in late May, spacing them 18 inches apart in deep soil blocks.
growthRequirements: Demands exceptionally well-drained loam or raised beds with a pH scale running from 6.0 to 6.8. Highly sensitive to cold clay or waterlogs. Requires deep, continuous watering cycles and absolute unshaded full sun.

whatToFeed: Organic fertilizer rich in magnesium and potassium; avoid excess nitrogen.
pruningTips: Pinch out the early first blossoms to encourage the plant to put energy into a stronger root system and structural branch system.
flavorProfile: Mildly sweet, bright, and exceptionally clean-tasting.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Start seeds indoors mid-March; transplant outdoors late May.
whenToHarvest: Harvest when the entire pepper shifts from green to a vibrant, uniform yellow color.
usesForLargeHarvests: Freezing, stir-fry prep bags, pickling rings, raw salad prep storage.
plantingInstructions: Sow indoors 1/4 inch deep in multi-cell trays 8-10 weeks prior to warm weather. Transplant outdoors into deeply amended ground when night temperatures consistently hover above 55°F, keeping 12-18 inches between bases.
growthRequirements: Requires rich, porous, free-draining sandy loam with high organic leaf compost modifications and a pH of 6.2 to 6.8. Regular watering is key to steady development. Demands long, unclouded hot sun fields.

whatToFeed: Organic fertilizer rich in magnesium and potassium; avoid excess nitrogen.
pruningTips: Pinch out the early first blossoms to encourage the plant to put energy into a stronger root system and structural branch system.
flavorProfile: Very sweet, light, and tangy with a refreshing crunch.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Start seeds indoors mid-March; transplant outdoors late May.
whenToHarvest: Harvest when fully matured to a deep, solid orange hue.
usesForLargeHarvests: Freezing, fresh salsas, roasting, pickling, dehydrated flakes.
plantingInstructions: Sow seeds indoors mid-March into quality compost matrix cells. Move young plants outside late May after soil fully warms. Space 15-18 inches apart, pressing the root soil down snugly to support heavy pepper branch extensions later.
growthRequirements: Thrives best in neutral, loose, nutrient-dense loam or raised beds with a pH range of 6.0 to 6.8. Demands constant moist conditions to circumvent blossom drop, combined with complete day-long raw sun access.

whatToFeed: Minimal nitrogen needed; uses soil inoculant to leverage nitrogen-fixing root nodules.
pruningTips: Do not prune heavily; simply clear away low-hanging spent yellow leaves post-harvest.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Direct sow outdoors from mid-May to early June after frost danger passes.
whenToHarvest: Pick when pods are firm, crisp, and the thickness of a pencil before seeds bulge.
usesForLargeHarvests: Canning, pressure-canning, freezing after blanching, pickled dilly beans.
plantingInstructions: Sow seeds directly into outdoor garden soil 1 inch deep, leaving 2-3 inches of distance between individual beans in a row. Keep row passages 18 inches apart. Do not transplant, as tender bush roots hate being handled.
growthRequirements: Prefers lightweight, moderately rich, highly well-draining sandy silt with a pH balance of 6.0 to 7.0. Requires steady watering routines during pod creation cycles, but hates soggy roots. Thrives under full sun.

whatToFeed: Light application of bone meal for phosphorus; avoid nitrogen.
pruningTips: Pinch the tender growing vine tips to use as gourmet salad greens and force bushier vining.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Direct sow outdoors in late March or early April as soon as soil can be worked.
whenToHarvest: Harvest when pods plump up fully but are still crisp and sweet.
usesForLargeHarvests: Blanching and freezing, stir-fry packs, quick snacking storage.
hardinessNote: Grow as a cool-season annual in Zone 6/7
plantingInstructions: Sow seeds straight into chilly spring beds 1 inch deep, spaced 2 inches apart along the footings of a wire mesh trellis or fence layout. Firm soil gently over the top to protect the seeds from hungry spring birds.
growthRequirements: Thrives beautifully in light, crumbly, cool loam containing heavy compost mixes and showing a neutral pH of 6.0 to 7.5. Demands reliable watering schedules and clear full sun, but accepts part shade in warmer weather.

whatToFeed: Rock phosphate and potassium-heavy liquid seaweed; avoid nitrogen to prevent hairy, split roots.
pruningTips: Thin young seedlings down meticulously to a 2-inch spacing rather than pruning tops.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Direct sow seeds outdoors from early-to-mid April; repeat in late summer for a fall harvest.
whenToHarvest: Pull from the ground when tops reach 0.5 to 1 inch in diameter at the soil line.
usesForLargeHarvests: Canning, pickling, root-cellar storage in sand, freezing coin cuts, carrot soups.
hardinessNote: Biennial grown as an annual in Zone 6/7
plantingInstructions: Sow tiny seeds outdoors 1/4 inch deep in ultra-fine, prepared soil beds, mixing with a splash of sand to dilute clusters. Keep soil continuously misted for 2-3 weeks until the delicate feathery sprouts break through.
growthRequirements: Requires deep, loose, stone-free sandy garden soils with top-tier internal drainage and a pH range of 6.0 to 6.8. Compaction causes forks and splits. Needs consistent watering cycles and unobstructed full sun.

whatToFeed: Grows too quickly to require feeding if planted in standard rich compost soil.
pruningTips: Thin seedlings immediately to 1 inch apart as soon as green tops emerge.
flavorProfile: Crisp, crunchy, and peppery with a sharp bite.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Direct sow early April through May, and again in late August for fall crops.
whenToHarvest: Pull out as soon as roots reach 1 inch in diameter; leaving them too long makes them woody/pithy.
usesForLargeHarvests: Pickling, roasting whole, radish top pesto, cold storage.
plantingInstructions: Sow seeds directly into soft topsoil 1/2 inch deep, spaced roughly 1 inch apart in neat rows 6-12 inches apart. Sow small quantities every 10 days in spring for consecutive continuous harvests through June.
growthRequirements: Flourishes in average, highly loose, completely non-compact loams with a near-neutral pH of 6.0 to 7.0. Requires fast, uninhibited growth matching steady moisture levels. Prefers full sun but works fine in part shade.

whatToFeed: High-nitrogen fish emulsion to encourage massive green leaf production.
pruningTips: Harvest the outer leaves continually to prolong vegetative production and delay seed bolting.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Direct sow outdoors in late March/early April, or late August for fall crops.
whenToHarvest: Cut outer leaves continuously once they are 3-4 inches long, or harvest full rosette before bolting.
usesForLargeHarvests: Blanching and freezing blocks, canning, dehydrating for green powder, spanakopita baking.
hardinessNote: Hardy cool-season annual; can overwinter with protection in Zone 6/7
plantingInstructions: Sow seeds directly outdoors in early spring 1/2 inch deep, dropping 12 seeds per foot of row. Space rows 1 foot apart. Thin established clusters down to a 4-6 inch gap between rosettes to prevent mold layout.
growthRequirements: Prefers rich, nitrogen-heavy, well-draining crumbly soils with a slightly alkaline pH range of 6.5 to 7.5; spinach hates overly sour profiles. Demands consistent moisture and likes partial shade as warm weather approaches.

pruningTips: Strip the bottom-most matured leaves using a downward motion, leaving the central crown to continue growing tall.
flavorProfile: Deeply earthy, nutty, and robust, turning sweet after a light frost.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Direct sow outdoors in mid-top-late April or early August for autumn crop.
whenToHarvest: Harvest individual outer leaves from the bottom up once they reach the size of your hand.
usesForLargeHarvests: Kale chips, blanching and freezing, hearty soup preps, juicing, canning.
hardinessNote: Biennial grown as annual; easily overwinters and sweetens with frost in Zone 6/7
plantingInstructions: Sow seeds directly into rows 1/4 inch deep, or tuck robust indoor nursery starts into the garden plot in mid-April. Space plants 12-18 inches apart to afford the blue-green tropical fronds room to fan outward.
growthRequirements: Thrives inside cool, organic-heavy, moisture-retentive loamy clay structures with a stable pH between 6.0 and 7.5. Demands uniform watering cycles. Prefers full sun, but handles partial afternoon shade effortlessly.

pruningTips: Pick outer wrapper leaves early or cut the entire head 1 inch above the base to let it regrow.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Direct sow outdoors early April through May, and again in late August.
whenToHarvest: Harvest the whole head once the center feels firm and well-packed.
usesForLargeHarvests: Lettuce wraps, chopped salads, gifting to neighbors, brief cold storage storage.
hardinessNote: Grow as a cool-season annual in Zone 6/7
plantingInstructions: Sow seeds 1/8 inch deep directly onto fine-tilled spring topsoil, or transplant tiny cell blocks with minimum root disruption. Space heads 8-10 inches apart in rows to let the soft round hearts develop without crowding.
growthRequirements: Requires shallow, highly loose, sandy loam loaded with rich compost amendments and a pH between 6.0 and 6.8. Root zones must stay continuously cool and hydrated; thrives best under partial afternoon shade to avoid bolting.
